title: Constrained emergency alternative water supply after internal continuity collapse
summary: Internal water continuity has collapsed, but an external emergency water supply is available within a usable window, although it is volume-limited and operationally burdensome.
suspected_issue: The greenhouse has already reached internal continuity collapse conditions. An emergency external tanker supply is confirmed and can arrive in time, but it will only provide a limited refill volume and requires a slow manual connection process. The service must decide whether this constrained recovery path justifies backup-oriented action or a more cautious recovery response.
symptoms: The main tank level is critically low and internal continuity is not credible without external help.
Hydraulic execution remains technically available, but internal reserves alone no longer justify continued irrigation.
An emergency tanker supply is confirmed within a usable time window, but available refill volume is limited.
The tanker connection requires manual setup and staged refill, creating operational burden and slower recovery than in a clean backup case.
Sector A remains the highest-priority sector if continuity is resumed under constrained recovery.
The service must decide whether this usable but imperfect external recovery path changes the recommendation relative to both the strong-backup case and the weak-backup case.

observed_signals.interpretation: Tank level is critically low and does not support credible internal continuity without external replenishment.
Flow remains technically available, but recovery would need to be staged carefully because refill and redistribution will not be immediate or abundant.
Pressure remains adequate enough to support a constrained recovery plan, but not a broad return to normal continuity.
Emergency supply is confirmed and usable, but refill volume is limited and connection effort will delay full recovery.

operational_context.note: Internal reserve-based continuity has already collapsed, but a usable external recovery path exists within a realistic decision window.
operational_context.impact: The service should distinguish this case from both the no-recovery collapse case and the strong-clean backup case.

operational_context.note: The emergency tanker connection is confirmed, but setup is manual and slower than a clean integrated backup path.
operational_context.impact: The service should consider whether constrained recovery supports backup-oriented action or a more cautious staged response.

operational_context.note: Available refill volume is not enough for a full return to normal irrigation and may support only limited selective recovery.
operational_context.impact: The service should reveal whether it can reason about recovery quality, not just recovery existence.

agronomic_context.note: Sector A remains the most sensitive sector and would be the first target for recovery irrigation if tanker supply is used.
agronomic_context.impact: The service should protect Sector A first if constrained recovery is judged worthwhile.

business_context.note: The emergency tanker option is expensive and operationally cumbersome, but may still preserve more value than treating the case as unrecoverable.
business_context.impact: The service should weigh constrained recovery value against the cost and burden of activating the backup path.

notes: This case is designed to test whether Agro-DO distinguishes a usable but operationally constrained recovery path from both a strong clean backup path and a weak delayed backup path.
